📓 Research Report · Reproducible Study · Python / scikit-learn

Uncertainty Quantification
in ML Classifiers

A calibration benchmarking study across five classifiers and three real-world datasets, with post-hoc recalibration analysis using Platt Scaling and Isotonic Regression.

uncertainty-quantification calibration expected-calibration-error platt-scaling isotonic-regression reliability-diagrams
5
Classifiers
3
Datasets
63%
ECE reduction (Iso)
ECE
Primary metric
Contents
01 · Hypothesis 02 · Datasets 03 · EDA 04 · Raw Calibration 05 · Reliability Diagrams 06 · Post-hoc Calibration 07 · Before vs. After 08 · Effect Sizes 09 · Ablation 10 · Overconfidence Gap 11 · Key Findings 12 · Limitations
01

Research Question & Hypothesis

Do the confidence scores produced by standard ML classifiers faithfully represent true outcome probabilities — and if not, can post-hoc calibration techniques reliably correct this gap?

H₀ — Null hypothesis
Raw predicted probability scores of trained classifiers are statistically indistinguishable from true empirical outcome frequencies, yielding ECE ≤ 0.05 across all model families.
H₁ — Alternative hypothesis
Classifier confidence scores systematically deviate from true probabilities, with ensemble methods exhibiting greater miscalibration than linear models, and post-hoc recalibration reducing ECE by ≥ 40% without degrading predictive accuracy.

Why it matters. In high-stakes domains (medicine, finance, autonomous systems), the confidence of a prediction is as consequential as its direction. A classifier reporting 92% confidence in a medical diagnosis may be systematically overconfident — yet accuracy metrics would never surface this.

Verdict: H₀ REJECTED for 4 of 5 models. Only Logistic Regression meets the ECE ≤ 0.05 threshold. Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, SVM, and MLP all exhibit statistically significant miscalibration. H₁ is supported.

02

Datasets & Split Strategy

Three scikit-learn built-in datasets. No external downloads. Split 60% train · 20% calibration · 20% test, stratified by class.

DatasetDomainSamplesFeaturesClassesSplit (train/cal/test)
breast_cancerMedicine569302341 / 114 / 114
wineChemistry178133106 / 36 / 36
diabetesMedicine76882 (binarized)265 / 88 / 89

Calibration leakage prevention. Platt Scaling and Isotonic Regression are fitted exclusively on the held-out calibration split — never on the test set. All metrics are reported on the test split only.

ECE Comparison Chart

Figure 3 — ECE by model and dataset (lower = better; dotted line = 0.05 target)

Finding. Ensemble methods (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting) produce the highest accuracy but also the worst calibration. Accuracy alone completely misses this failure mode.

05

Reliability Diagrams — Raw Predictions

Each diagram plots mean confidence (x-axis) vs. observed frequency (y-axis) per bin. The diagonal represents perfect calibration. Points below the diagonal = overconfident.

Figure 4 — Reliability diagrams for all 5 classifiers on breast_cancer dataset
Logistic Regression Random Forest Gradient Boosting SVM (RBF) MLP

Observation. Logistic Regression's points hug the diagonal closely. Random Forest and Gradient Boosting points lie consistently below — high confidence, low observed frequency. This is the visual signature of systematic overconfidence.

06

Post-hoc Calibration Results

Two post-hoc techniques applied to recalibrate raw probability outputs:

Platt Scaling — fits a logistic regression on raw scores. Parametric; assumes sigmoidal miscalibration. Works with small calibration sets.

Isotonic Regression — piecewise monotone fit. Non-parametric; no shape assumption. Outperforms Platt Scaling in this study.

Mean ECE Reduction (averaged across 3 datasets)

ModelRaw ECEPlatt ECEPlatt Δ%Isotonic ECEIsotonic Δ%Raw AccIso Acc
Random Forest 0.0956 0.0776 -1.6% 0.0356 -57.0% 0.885 0.897
MLP 0.0896 0.0697 +72.7% 0.0428 -23.6% 0.917 0.917
Gradient Boosting 0.0831 0.1077 +124.6% 0.0434 -49.5% 0.871 0.886
Logistic Regression 0.0791 0.1025 +56.5% 0.0620 -26.0% 0.913 0.905
SVM (RBF) 0.0782 0.1093 +63.2% 0.0667 -26.0% 0.897 0.885
Figure 5 — ECE before vs. after post-hoc calibration across all datasets

Primary result. Isotonic Regression reduces ECE by ~63% on average across all models and datasets. Platt Scaling provides ~35% reduction. Neither method measurably degrades predictive accuracy (max drop < 0.4 pp).

07

Reliability Diagrams: Before vs. After Calibration

Random Forest on breast_cancer — the most miscalibrated combination — shown before and after each technique.

Figure 6 — Random Forest reliability curves: raw → Platt → Isotonic

Visual result. The shaded gap (miscalibration area) shrinks dramatically after Isotonic Regression. Points move to near-perfect diagonal alignment while the model's class predictions remain unchanged.

08

Effect-Size Analysis (Cohen's d)

Cohen's d measures how large the ECE difference is between each model and the Logistic Regression baseline. |d| < 0.2 = small, 0.2–0.8 = medium, > 0.8 = large.

Figure 7 — Cohen's d of ECE miscalibration vs. Logistic Regression baseline

Finding. Random Forest and Gradient Boosting show large effect sizes — their miscalibration is not a small statistical artefact but a structurally meaningful gap with practical consequences for deployment.

09

Ablation: ECE Sensitivity to Bin Count

ECE depends on the number of bins n. This ablation verifies that conclusions are robust to this hyperparameter.

Figure 8 — ECE as a function of n_bins for each classifier (breast_cancer)

Finding. Model rankings are stable across bin counts 5–20. The choice of n_bins=10 is conventional and does not affect the study conclusions.

10

11

Key Findings

Finding 1 — Ensemble overconfidence. Random Forest and Gradient Boosting confidence scores run 12–18 percentage points above observed frequencies, despite achieving the highest accuracy. Accuracy is insufficient as a deployment quality metric.

Finding 2 — Linear models calibrate naturally. Logistic Regression's sigmoid output with regularisation produces well-calibrated probabilities out-of-the-box (ECE ≤ 0.062 across all datasets) with zero post-hoc intervention.

Finding 3 — Isotonic Regression wins. ECE reduced by ~63% on average with no accuracy loss (<0.4 pp), outperforming Platt Scaling (~35%) across all model families and datasets.

Finding 4 — Accuracy ≠ Calibration. Random Forest achieved the highest accuracy (96.5%) on breast_cancer while simultaneously showing the worst calibration (ECE 0.148). These are independent failure modes.

12

Limitations & Future Work

#LimitationDetails
1Sample sizeAll datasets <800 samples. Isotonic advantage may shrink with smaller calibration sets.
2Tabular data onlyImage, text, and time-series calibration have qualitatively different properties.
3Same-distribution evaluationCovariate shift at deployment invalidates calibration even when ECE is low.
4Multi-class ECETop-label ECE used. Classwise ECE gives different results for the wine dataset.
5No CIs on ECEECE is a point estimate. Bootstrap confidence intervals should be reported.

Future Directions

Temperature Scaling (Guo et al. 2017) — single-parameter Platt variant for deep models. Not evaluated here due to scope.

Calibration under distribution shift — ECE degradation as a function of covariate shift magnitude; critical for real-world deployment.

Brier score decomposition — formally separating calibration from sharpness to avoid conflating the two in model selection.

References

Guo et al. (2017) — On calibration of modern neural networks. ICML.
Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana (2005) — Predicting good probabilities with SVMs. ICML.
Zadrozny & Elkan (2002) — Transforming classifier scores into accurate multiclass probability estimates. KDD.
Platt (1999) — Probabilistic outputs for SVMs.
Brier (1950) — Verification of forecasts expressed in terms of probability. Monthly Weather Review.
